critic_raw:
This is a well-constructed experimental design. The chronological split is correctly executed, leakage controls are articulated clearly, the baseline is legitimately competitive (logistic regression on Group B features with equivalent tuning effort plus the flattened-sequence ablation), and the metric choice is defensible for the class imbalance regime. The scope section is appropriately honest about what can and cannot be concluded. Let me go through the areas where I see genuine methodological tension.

**Issue 1: The bidirectional LSTM is acausal at serving time — but the flaw goes deeper than that**

The methodology proposes a Bidirectional LSTM. In sequence fraud detection the standard objection is that BiLSTM uses 'future' events within the 14-day window (i.e., events that occurred after an earlier event in the same window but before the transaction itself). This is not future leakage relative to the transaction timestamp — all window events are prior to the transaction — but it raises a prerequisite assumption: the model implicitly assumes that the full pre-transaction activity window is available at serving time. In production, if event ingestion has any real-time latency (delayed login events, out-of-order transaction confirmations from partner banks), the sequence available at scoring time may differ from the sequence seen during training. The bidirectional architecture is particularly sensitive to this because it conditions each position on both past and future events within the window; a missing event near the beginning of the window affects representations at all positions. The methodology does not specify whether window contents are treated as a point-in-time snapshot or whether they can be backfilled. This is not a labeling leakage problem — it is a silent deployment mismatch: the model trains on clean, complete sequences but may score on incomplete ones, and AUCPR on the held-out test set would not detect this because the test set is constructed from historical data with the same completeness guarantee as training.

What would constitute evidence: Compare model AUCPR on test sequences with artificially induced missingness at varying rates (5%, 15%, 30% of events dropped or delayed) against the baseline under the same degradation. If the sequence model degrades faster than the logistic regression baseline, the operational advantage is fragile. Alternatively, if the methodology commits to causal (forward-only) LSTMs or masks attention to causal positions only, this concern is substantially reduced.

**Issue 2: The 'retrain on train+validation combined with early stopping on held-out 10% of validation' is procedurally ambiguous and potentially optimistic**

The methodology states: 'The final model is retrained on train+validation combined, using early stopping on a held-out 10% of validation to prevent overfitting.' This means 10% of the validation months (Months 13–18) are withheld from final training to serve as an early-stopping signal. The hyperparameters were selected on the full validation set AUCPR. Then the model is retrained on train + 90% of validation, with early stopping on the remaining 10%. Several problems:

(a) The 10% early-stopping slice is necessarily the most recent part of the validation window (if chronological order is preserved) or a random slice (which would introduce leakage by training on months 14–18 while stopping on month 13 data). The methodology does not specify which, and these have opposite biases — chronological holdout is conservative; random holdout leaks future sequence information into the stopping signal.

(b) Hyperparameters were tuned on the full validation set, including the 10% that is now used for early stopping. The early-stopping slice is not truly independent of hyperparameter selection. This inflates confidence that the final model is well-calibrated.

(c) The baseline (logistic regression) does not have an equivalent early-stopping procedure — it is retrained on train+validation without this holdout. This asymmetry means the sequence model has an additional regularization mechanism that the baseline lacks, confounding the architectural comparison.

What would constitute evidence: Specify that the early-stopping 10% is always the final chronological slice of the validation period. Report final test AUCPR both with and without the retrain-on-combined step (i.e., also report the validation-tuned model's test performance without retraining) to verify that retraining improves rather than degrades performance. Apply an equivalent procedure to the baseline if any form of combined retraining is used.

**Issue 3: The 'disagreement resolution' rule (prefer simpler model if validation and test winners disagree) is a form of implicit test set use**

The methodology states: 'If the validation winner and the test-window winner disagree, the simpler configuration is preferred for final retraining on train+validation to reduce the chance of selecting a model that is too specialized to the validation slice. Final AUCPR and secondary metrics are then reported on that same held-out test window.'

This is a conditional decision rule that uses the test set outcome to influence model selection, and then reports AUCPR on that same test set. Even if the decision is always 'prefer simpler,' the act of checking test-set disagreement and potentially changing the selected model based on it means the test AUCPR is no longer from a single pre-committed evaluation — it reflects a two-stage selection process where the test set participates in the second stage. The reported confidence intervals (via bootstrap) will not capture this selection effect; bootstrap CIs on the final test AUCPR assume the model was fixed before the test set was examined.

This is a subtle but real form of adaptive test-set use. The mechanism of failure: if validation AUCPR overestimates the sequence model's performance on the test period (because the validation slice happens to be particularly favorable to temporal features), the disagreement check triggers the fallback to the simpler model. The simpler model's test AUCPR is then reported as the final result — but that model was selected partly because its test performance looked better. In expectation, this procedure will underestimate the simple model's error and overestimate the reliability of the reported AUCPR.

What would constitute evidence: Pre-commit to a single model selection rule before examining the test set. If the disagreement check is retained as a robustness measure, report both models' test AUCPRs transparently and do not use the test outcome to determine which is 'final.' Alternatively, reserve a fourth temporal window (Months 25–28 if available) as a true holdout, using Months 19–24 for the disagreement check without reporting it as the final evaluation.

**Issue 4: The asymmetric window lengths (14-day sequence vs. 30-day aggregate) are presented as a control but are not**

The methodology claims: 'Both models encode recent activity (baseline: 30-day window; sequence: 14-day), so improvements come from temporal ordering, not just looking more recently.' This framing inverts the standard concern. The baseline sees more history (30 days of aggregates) than the sequence model (14 days of events). If the sequence model wins, the methodology attributes this to temporal ordering — but the 14-day sequence may simply capture high-density recent behavior that the 30-day aggregate dilutes by averaging over a longer window. Conversely, if the baseline wins, it might be because 30 days of aggregate context dominates the shorter sequential view.

The secondary ablation (logistic regression on flattened Group A features) partially addresses this — if flattened 14-day features outperform the 30-day aggregate baseline, that would isolate recency from ordering. But this ablation is described as a 'confirmation' rather than a primary comparison, and the methodology does not specify whether a 30-day sequence model is tested, which would be the cleanest control for window length.

What would constitute evidence: Add a 30-day sequence model condition and a 14-day aggregate condition. Without these, AUCPR differences between the sequence model and the baseline are confounded by window length, and the stated conclusion ('improvements come from temporal ordering') is not fully supported by the design.

**Summary:**

Issues 1 and 3 are the most methodologically serious. Issue 1 (bidirectional architecture with implicit completeness assumption) is a silent misconfiguration risk that test-set AUCPR cannot detect. Issue 3 (conditional model selection using test set outcome then reporting AUCPR on that set) is an adaptive inference problem that invalidates the reported confidence intervals. Issues 2 and 4 are real but more addressable within the current design. The core comparison — sequence model vs. feature-engineered baseline under chronological split with AUCPR — is sound in principle; the problems are in specific procedural choices around the final model selection and evaluation.
all_issues_raised: The bidirectional LSTM implicitly assumes complete, low-latency event windows at serving time; partial or delayed windows at inference would degrade the sequence model in ways the held-out test AUCPR cannot detect.

The early-stopping procedure on 10% of validation is procedurally underspecified (chronological vs. random slice), shares data with hyperparameter selection, and is asymmetric relative to the baseline — confounding the architectural comparison.

The conditional disagreement-resolution rule uses the test set outcome to influence final model selection and then reports AUCPR on that same test set, invalidating the reported confidence intervals as a measure of pre-committed generalization.

The 14-day sequence window vs. 30-day aggregate window lengths are framed as a control for recency but are not equal, conflating window length with temporal ordering as explanations for any AUCPR difference.
all_issues_adjudicated: The bidirectional LSTM implicitly assumes complete, low-latency event windows at serving time; partial or delayed windows at inference would degrade the sequence model in ways the held-out test AUCPR cannot detect.

The early-stopping procedure on 10% of validation is procedurally underspecified (chronological vs. random slice), shares data with hyperparameter selection, and is asymmetric relative to the baseline — confounding the architectural comparison.

The conditional disagreement-resolution rule uses the test set outcome to influence final model selection and then reports AUCPR on that same test set, invalidating the reported confidence intervals as a measure of pre-committed generalization.

The 14-day sequence window vs. 30-day aggregate window lengths are framed as a control for recency but are not equal, conflating window length with temporal ordering as explanations for any AUCPR difference.
